abstract: Extracting structured knowledge from scientific literature remains a significant challenge for machine learning models due to the density and complexity of the text. In this work, we present a straightforward approach to joint named entity recognition and relation extraction by fine-tuning pretrained large language models, specifically GPT-3 and Llama-2, to identify and categorize complex scientific data. We evaluate this method across three representative tasks in materials chemistry: linking dopants to host materials, cataloging metal-organic frameworks, and extracting general information regarding material compositions, phases, morphologies, and applications. Our models process information from both individual sentences and full paragraphs, yielding outputs in flexible formats ranging from natural language sentences to structured JSON objects. This methodology offers a highly accessible and versatile route for building large-scale, specialized databases of structured knowledge directly from research papers.
